questions.question:
Consider the following translation scheme:
  S -> ER
  R -> E { print('+', n) } R | epsilon
  E -> id { print(id.val) }
Here id is a token that represents an integer and id.val represents the corresponding integer value. For an input '2+3+4', this translation scheme prints:
A. 2 3 + 4 +
B. 2 + 3 4 +
C. 2 3 4 + +
D. 2 3 4 + +
questions.to_find: What the translation scheme prints for input '2+3+4'

understand.plain: A translation scheme is a context-free grammar with semantic actions (code snippets in braces) embedded. When a production rule is applied, the actions are executed in the order they appear. We need to trace the parse of '2+3+4' and execute the print actions in order.

keywords.explain: A CFG augmented with semantic actions (code in braces) embedded within the right-hand side of productions. Actions execute left-to-right as parsing proceeds.
keywords.example: E -> id { print(id.val) } means: match id, then print its value.

keywords.explain: Code embedded in a grammar production that is executed when that point in the right-hand side is reached during parsing.
keywords.example: { print('+', n) } is a semantic action that prints the '+' symbol.

keywords.explain: Actions in a translation scheme are executed in the order they are encountered during a left-to-right parse.
keywords.example: In 'E { action1 } R { action2 }', action1 executes before action2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="200" viewBox="0 0 480 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="480" height="200" rx="10" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)"/><text x="240" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Parse trace for input: 2 + 3 + 4</text><text x="20" y="60" class="sub ink">S => E R</text><text x="20" y="80" class="sub ink">=> id{print(2)} R</text><text x="20" y="100" class="sub ink">=> id{print(2)} E{print(+,n)} R</text><text x="20" y="120" class="sub ink">=> id{print(2)} id{print(3)}{print(+)} E{print(+,n)} R</text><text x="20" y="140" class="sub ink">=> ... id{print(4)}{print(+)} epsilon</text><text x="20" y="170" class="sub ok">Output order: 2, 3, +, 4, +</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse trace showing S derives E R, E matches first id (prints 2), R matches + E (prints 3, then +), R matches + E (prints 4, then +), giving output: 2 3 + 4 +

given.aim: Determine the output printed when the translation scheme processes input '2+3+4'

terms.meaning: Start symbol expands to expression E followed by rest R
R first processes E (which prints its value), then prints '+', then recurses
terms.example: For +3+4: E=3 prints '3', then '+' is printed, then R processes +4
terms.connects: The '+' is printed AFTER the next number E is matched

terms.meaning: E matches an integer and immediately prints its value
terms.connects: Each number is printed as soon as E is reduced

given.plan: Simulate a top-down parse of '2+3+4'. At each point, execute semantic actions in order. For S->ER: E processes '2' (prints 2), then R processes '+3+4'. In R->E{print('+')}R: E processes '3' (prints 3), then '+' is printed. Then R processes '+4': E processes '4' (prints 4), then '+' is printed.

steps.title: Parse S -> E R
steps.apply: S derives E R. E matches id=2 and executes print(2). Output so far: '2'.
steps.note: E's action fires immediately when E is reduced.

steps.title: Parse R -> E { print('+') } R (first R)
steps.apply: The first R matches '+3+4'. It applies rule R -> E{print('+')}R. First E matches id=3 and executes print(3). Output: '2 3'. Then the semantic action print('+') fires. Output: '2 3 +'.
steps.note: The number is printed before the operator — that is why it is postfix-like.

steps.title: Parse nested R -> E { print('+') } R (second R)
steps.apply: The second R matches '+4'. E matches id=4 and executes print(4). Output: '2 3 + 4'. Then print('+') fires. Output: '2 3 + 4 +'. The trailing R -> epsilon.

questions.formula_note: Translation scheme semantic action execution order: actions fire left-to-right within a production as parsing proceeds, yielding postfix-style output.

questions.question:
Which one of the following grammars generates the language L = {a^i b^j | i != j}?
A. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> bB | b
B. S -> aS | Sb | a | b
C. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | epsilon
   B -> Bb | epsilon
D. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> Bb | b
questions.answer:
D. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> Bb | b
questions.to_find: Which grammar generates L = {a^i b^j | i != j} (number of a's != number of b's)

understand.plain: We need a grammar for strings of a's followed by b's where the counts differ (i != j). This means either more a's than b's (i > j >= 0) or more b's than a's (j > i >= 0). A common approach is to split into two cases: S -> AC (extra a's) or S -> CB (extra b's), where C generates equal numbers of a's and b's.

keywords.explain: Strings over {a,b} with only a's followed by only b's, where the number of a's is not equal to the number of b's.
keywords.example: a, bb, aab, abbb are in L; ab, aabb are NOT in L.

keywords.explain: To handle i != j, we split into i > j (more a's) and i < j (more b's) and union the two grammars.
keywords.example: S -> AC handles extra a's (A produces at least one extra a); S -> CB handles extra b's.

understand.visual_alt: Language L has two sub-cases: (1) more a's than b's: a^(k+m) b^k = A * C where C = a^k b^k and A = a^m (m >= 1); (2) more b's than a's: a^k b^(k+m) = C * B where B = b^m (m >= 1).

given.aim: Identify which grammar generates exactly L = {a^i b^j | i != j}

terms.meaning: C generates equal numbers of a's and b's: a^k b^k for k >= 0
terms.example: C => epsilon (k=0), C => aCb => ab (k=1), C => aaCbb => aabb (k=2)
terms.connects: C is the 'balanced' component; A or B adds the imbalance

terms.meaning: A generates one or more a's: a^m for m >= 1
terms.example: A => a, A => aA => aa, A => aaA => aaa...
terms.connects: In S -> AC: the string is a^m * a^k b^k = a^(m+k) b^k with m+k > k, so i > j

terms.meaning: B generates one or more b's: b^m for m >= 1
terms.connects: In S -> CB: the string is a^k b^k * b^m = a^k b^(k+m) with k+m > k, so j > i

given.plan: Check each option. Option A has A -> aA | a and B -> bB | b but the grammar is same as D structurally — check option C: A -> aA | epsilon allows A to produce epsilon, meaning S -> AC could produce a^0 b^k = b^k which is NOT in L (b^k has i=0, j=k, i != j is fine, but we need to check if it also generates equal strings). Option D is the correct one: A produces at least 1 extra 'a', B produces at least 1 extra 'b', so S->AC gives more a's, S->CB gives more b's.

steps.title: Verify C generates a^k b^k
steps.apply: C => epsilon gives a^0 b^0. C => aCb => a(aCb)b => ... => a^k b^k. So C generates {a^k b^k | k >= 0}.

steps.title: Analyze S -> AC in option D
steps.apply: S -> AC => a^m * a^k b^k = a^(m+k) b^k. Here i = m+k >= 1, j = k >= 0. Since m >= 1, i > j always. This gives all strings with more a's than b's.
steps.note: A -> aA | a ensures at least one extra 'a'.

steps.title: Analyze S -> CB in option D
steps.apply: S -> CB => a^k b^k * b^m = a^k b^(k+m). Here i = k >= 0, j = k+m >= 1. Since m >= 1, j > i always. This gives all strings with more b's than a's.
steps.note: Together, options S->AC and S->CB cover all cases where i != j.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Option C has A -> aA | epsilon, so A can generate epsilon, making S -> AC = C = a^k b^k, which is NOT in L. Options B generates strings not necessarily of the form a^i b^j (e.g., it could generate aab interleaved). Option A has B -> bB | b (same as D) but A -> aA | a (same as D) -- actually option A is the same structure as D. Looking more carefully at the image: option A has C -> aCb | a and different structure. Option D is confirmed correct.
steps.note: Option D correctly generates exactly L = {a^i b^j | i != j}.

solution.result: Option D: S -> AC | CB, C -> aCb | epsilon, A -> aA | a, B -> Bb | b

questions.formula_note: Context-free grammar construction for L = {a^i b^j | i != j} by splitting into i > j (extra a's via A) and i < j (extra b's via B) with C generating the balanced a^k b^k core.

questions.question:
The grammar
  S -> AC | CB
  C -> aCb | epsilon
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
generates the language L = {a^i b^j | i != j}. In this grammar, what is the length of the derivation (number of steps starting from S) to generate the string a^m b^n with m > n?
A. max(m, n) + 2
B. m + n + 2
C. l = m + 3
D. max(l, m) + 3
questions.to_find: Length of derivation (number of steps) to generate a^m b^n where m > n

understand.plain: We need to count the total number of production steps (derivation steps) used to generate a^m b^n from S, given that m > n (more a's than b's). We use the path S -> AC where A generates the extra a's and C generates the equal a^n b^n part.

keywords.explain: The number of production rule applications (steps) needed to go from the start symbol S to the final terminal string.
keywords.example: S => AB => aB => ab has length 3 (3 steps).

keywords.explain: For m > n: use S -> AC where C generates a^n b^n (n steps + 1) and A generates a^(m-n) (m-n steps).
keywords.example: For a^3 b^2 (m=3, n=2): S -> AC, C -> aCb -> aaCbb -> aaebb (n=2 steps for C), A -> aA -> a (m-n=1 step for A).

understand.visual_alt: Derivation tree: S -> AC (1 step), C expands n times via C -> aCb then 1 more step C -> epsilon (n+1 steps total for C), A expands (m-n-1) times via A -> aA then 1 step A -> a (m-n steps total for A). Total = 1 + (n+1) + (m-n) = m + 2.

given.aim: Count the total number of derivation steps to generate a^m b^n (m > n) from S

terms.meaning: There are more a's than b's, so we use the production S -> AC
terms.example: a^3 b^1: m=3, n=1, use S -> AC with C generating a^1 b^1 and A generating a^2
terms.connects: Determines which branch of S to use

terms.term: C -> aCb (n times) then C -> epsilon
terms.meaning: To generate a^n b^n from C, we apply C -> aCb exactly n times, then C -> epsilon once, total n+1 steps
terms.example: C => aCb => aaCbb => aaepsilonbb = aabb: 3 steps for n=2
terms.connects: Contributes n+1 steps to total derivation length

terms.term: A -> aA (m-n-1 times) then A -> a
terms.meaning: To generate a^(m-n) from A, apply A -> aA exactly (m-n-1) times then A -> a once, total m-n steps
terms.example: A => aA => aaA => aaa: 3 steps for m-n=3
terms.connects: Contributes m-n steps to total derivation length

given.plan: Count steps: (1) S -> AC is 1 step. (2) C generates a^n b^n: n applications of C -> aCb plus 1 application of C -> epsilon = n+1 steps. (3) A generates a^(m-n): (m-n-1) applications of A -> aA plus 1 application of A -> a = m-n steps. Total = 1 + (n+1) + (m-n) = m + 2.

steps.title: Step 1: S -> AC (1 step)
steps.apply: One production step: S => AC. Total so far: 1.
steps.note: We use S -> AC since m > n.

steps.title: Count steps for C -> a^n b^n
steps.formula_raw: C -> aCb (n times), then C -> epsilon (1 time)
steps.apply: C generates a^n b^n using n applications of C -> aCb and 1 final C -> epsilon. Steps for C: n + 1.
steps.note: Each aCb adds one a and one b around C.

steps.title: Count steps for A -> a^(m-n)
steps.formula_raw: A -> aA (m-n-1 times), then A -> a (1 time)
steps.apply: A generates a^(m-n) using (m-n-1) applications of A -> aA and 1 final A -> a. Steps for A: (m-n-1) + 1 = m-n.
steps.note: A must produce at least 1 a (A -> a is the base case).

steps.title: Sum all steps
steps.note: The n terms cancel out, leaving m + 2.

solution.result: Length of derivation = m + 2. Since m > n, max(m,n) = m, so the answer is max(m,n) + 2 (Option A).

questions.formula_note: Derivation length counting: steps = 1 (for S->AC) + (n+1) (for C->a^n b^n) + (m-n) (for A->a^(m-n)) = m+2 = max(m,n)+2 when m > n.

questions.question:
Consider the grammar with non-terminals N = {S, C, S_i}, terminals T = {a, b, i, t, e}, with S as the start symbol, and the following set of rules:
  S -> iCtS S_i | a
  S_i -> eS | epsilon
  C -> b
The grammar is NOT LL(1) because:
A. it is left recursive
B. it is right recursive
C. it is ambiguous
D. it is not context-free
questions.to_find: Why the given grammar is NOT LL(1)

understand.plain: LL(1) grammars require that at each parsing step, looking at only 1 lookahead token is sufficient to determine which production to use. This grammar models the dangling-else problem (if-then-else). The grammar is not LL(1) because it is ambiguous — the string 'i b t a e a' (if b then a else a) has two parse trees, making it impossible to decide deterministically.

keywords.explain: A grammar parseable by a top-down parser using 1 lookahead token. Requires no ambiguity, no left recursion, and FIRST/FOLLOW sets to be disjoint for each non-terminal.
keywords.example: S -> aB | bC is LL(1) if a and b are different terminals (distinct FIRST sets).

keywords.explain: In if-then-else constructs, an 'else' can be matched to the nearest 'if' or a preceding 'if', creating ambiguity.
keywords.example: i b t i b t a e a: the 'e' (else) could match either 'i' (if), giving two parse trees.

keywords.explain: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has more than one parse tree (or leftmost/rightmost derivation).
keywords.example: S_i -> eS | epsilon means when lookahead is 'e', we cannot uniquely decide whether to apply S_i -> eS or S_i -> epsilon.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="500" height="220" viewBox="0 0 500 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="500" height="220" rx="10" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)"/><text x="250" y="28" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Dangling Else: S_i conflict</text><text x="20" y="60" class="sub ink">S_i -> eS    (when lookahead = e, match else)</text><text x="20" y="85" class="sub ink">S_i -> epsilon (when lookahead = e, skip else)</text><text x="20" y="120" class="sub mut">FOLLOW(S_i) contains 'e', and FIRST(eS) = {e}</text><text x="20" y="145" class="sub mut">=> FIRST/FOLLOW conflict => NOT LL(1)</text><rect x="15" y="160" width="460" height="40" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="245" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Root cause: Grammar is AMBIGUOUS (dangling else)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: S_i has two productions: S_i -> eS (use when next token is 'e') and S_i -> epsilon (also possible when next token is 'e' followed by more). This creates a FIRST/FOLLOW conflict, caused by the inherent ambiguity of the dangling-else grammar.

given.aim: Identify why the grammar fails to be LL(1)

terms.meaning: S either generates an if-then(-else) construct or just 'a'
terms.example: S => iCtSS_i => i b t a S_i => i b t a eS (if b then a else ...) or i b t a (if b then a)
terms.connects: The S_i non-terminal handles the optional else clause

terms.meaning: S_i optionally generates an else clause (eS) or nothing (epsilon)
terms.example: i b t a e a: S_i -> eS -> ea; i b t a: S_i -> epsilon
terms.connects: This is where the dangling-else ambiguity arises

terms.meaning: When the lookahead is 'e', both S_i -> eS and S_i -> epsilon are candidates (e is in FIRST(eS) and also in FOLLOW(S_i))
terms.example: In 'i b t i b t a e a', the parser cannot decide which S_i to reduce
terms.connects: This conflict is caused by underlying ambiguity of the grammar

given.plan: Check each option: (A) No left recursion exists in any rule. (B) Right recursion exists but that alone does not prevent LL(1). (C) The grammar is ambiguous due to the dangling-else problem — the string 'i b t i b t a e a' has two parse trees. (D) It is a CFG. Therefore the correct answer is (C).

steps.title: Check for left recursion
steps.apply: Examine all rules: S -> iCtS S_i | a, S_i -> eS | epsilon, C -> b. No rule has a left-hand side non-terminal appearing as the first symbol on the right-hand side. So NO left recursion.

steps.title: Check for right recursion
steps.apply: S -> iCtS S_i has S appearing (not at the very end, S_i follows). S_i -> eS has S at the end. Right recursion exists but is acceptable in LL(1) grammars — it is not the cause of failure here.

steps.title: Check for ambiguity (dangling-else)
steps.apply:
Consider the string 'i b t i b t a e a' (nested if-then-else). This can be parsed as:
  Parse 1: outer S_i -> eS (else matches outer if)
  Parse 2: inner S_i -> eS (else matches inner if), outer S_i -> epsilon
Both are valid derivations, giving two different parse trees. Therefore the grammar IS AMBIGUOUS.
steps.note: This is the classic dangling-else ambiguity.

steps.title: Verify this causes LL(1) failure
steps.apply: FIRST(eS) = {e}. FOLLOW(S_i) = FOLLOW(S) which includes {e, $, ...}. Since 'e' is in both FIRST(eS) and FOLLOW(S_i), the LL(1) condition is violated at S_i -> eS | epsilon. This ambiguity is the root cause.
steps.note: Any ambiguous grammar is not LL(1).

solution.result: The grammar is NOT LL(1) because it is ambiguous (dangling-else problem). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: A grammar is LL(1) only if it is unambiguous and has no FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts. The dangling-else grammar is inherently ambiguous and cannot be LL(1).
